severityBands.label: Ordinary / expected
severityBands.guidance: The modal adjustment. An ordinary turn that does what a competent counsellor would do at this moment — plain explaining, a short factual answer, a normal discovery question, a routine acknowledgement — scores 0. Most turns score 0.

severityBands.label: Concrete relevant answer
severityBands.guidance: The counsellor gives a concrete, correct, relevant answer to what the student JUST asked — a specific fee figure, the real refund window, the actual schedule, a straight yes/no with the reason. Answering the question they asked, with a real answer, is worth +2 — it is NOT an ordinary 0.

severityBands.label: Addresses a concern/objection
severityBands.guidance: The counsellor directly and substantively addresses a concern or objection the student has raised this call, using specifics — numbers, proof, policy detail, and/or genuine empathy (e.g. explains the real refund terms and what triggers a refund, breaks down the fee, gives concrete placement stats with eligibility mechanics). This is the workhorse positive band when the student is pushing back and the counsellor actually engages the substance.

severityBands.label: Outstanding move
severityBands.guidance: An outstanding objection-handling move: decomposes the fee into seat-block-now + EMI, offers to bring a parent onto the call, screen-shares live proof, or lands a concrete agreed next step right after handling the objection. The kind of move that visibly turns a resistant student around.

severityBands.label: Clearly weak
severityBands.guidance: Vague where specifics were called for, ignores what the student just said, a rambling info-dump, talks over a question the student actually asked.

severityBands.label: Notably bad
severityBands.guidance: Pressure tactics on a hesitant student (manufactured deadline, 'decide right now', 'seats running out' used as a club rather than a fact), dismissing or belittling a stated concern, fake urgency on a trust objection.

severityBands.label: Rare extremes
severityBands.guidance: Reserve for genuine extremes only. Positive: a textbook close of a genuinely difficult, resistant call (+8..+10). Negative: lying about the programme, aggressive bullying, or browbeating a clearly uncomfortable student (-7..-10).

phaseExpectations.1: Opening. Good now: warm greeting, audibility/camera check, a short self-introduction, congratulating the student on being shortlisted, light agenda-setting. Listening and building rapport is the expected default and scores 0. Hard-pitching the course or jumping to fees this early reads as weak.
phaseExpectations.2: Discovery. This is where the student ANSWERS the counsellor's questions. Good now: short, genuine questions about background, current work/study, goals, why they applied, whether parents know. Letting the student talk is the expected default and scores 0. Lecturing or brochure-dumping instead of probing is weak.
phaseExpectations.3: Presentation. The counsellor is explaining the programme (curriculum, faculty, placements, certification, selectivity) while the student mostly LISTENS and gives short acknowledgements. Plain, clear explaining is the EXPECTED DEFAULT here and scores 0. Good: specifics tied to what THIS student said in discovery. Weak: 100+ word brochure dumps with no link to the student, lecturing without ever checking in.
phaseExpectations.4: Objections & Negotiation. This is where the student's questions and pushback concentrate (fees, parents, time, scholarships, placement scepticism, EMI). Good now: address the student's SPECIFIC objection with concrete information and empathy, then check back — decompose the fee, quote EMI tenures, offer a parent call, give the real recordings/attendance answer. Dismissing, brushing off, or pressuring a hesitant student is a serious fault.
phaseExpectations.5: Close. Good now: a clear, LOW-pressure next step — the 4,000 seat-blocking amount framed honestly, a dashboard/UPI walkthrough, a follow-up or callback if the student isn't ready. Pressure tactics on a still-hesitant student (last day, decide right now, seats running out as a club) are a serious fault.

reward: Decomposing the fee: a small seat-block amount now, the balance moved to EMI / a finance team — instead of quoting the full figure as a wall.
Quoting a concrete EMI tenure and monthly figure rather than a vague 'EMI is available'.
Inviting a parent onto the call to discuss fees and approval, instead of arguing alone with the student.
Answering the recordings-count-for-attendance / can-I-catch-up worry with the real policy (sessions are recorded, attendance is flexible).
Offering live screen-share / dashboard proof of curriculum, placement data, or the payment flow.
penalize: Fake urgency or invented deadlines ('today is the last day') used to pressure rather than inform.
Scarcity pressure ('only 70 seats left') aimed at a trust or affordability objection.
Ignoring or talking past the specific objection the student just raised.

guidelines: 0 is the modal value for genuinely ordinary turns, but the positive bands are REAL and must be used when earned: +2 = a concrete, correct, relevant answer to what the student just asked; +3..+4 = directly and substantively addressing a concern/objection the student raised (specifics, numbers, proof, empathy); +5..+7 = an outstanding move (decompose the fee, offer a parent call, concrete next step after handling an objection). Good counselling and persistence MUST move the meter up — never default an objection-handling turn to 0.

addressedObjection: the scorer also returns addressedObjection — an objection CATEGORY KEY (from server/data/seed/objections.json: fee, emi_affordability, parents_family, time_commitment, competing_priorities, trust_legitimacy, job_guarantee_placement, course_fit_relevance, language_english, tech_access) or 'other', else null. It is set when the counsellor gave a substantive, specific response to a concern the student has already raised this call (visible in the last-6-turns context); a brush-off, fake urgency, or bare policy restatement does NOT count. Backchannels always return null.

addressedObjection key-matching: the scoring prompt is fed the session's currently-OPEN objection keys and the scorer is told to return one of THOSE exact keys when the turn answers an open concern — so the session resolves the same concern it is tracking (without this, the scorer's free-pick key often mismatched the regex-assigned key and resolveObjection() no-opped, stranding the concern open forever and starving the 'ANSWERED — do not repeat' student-prompt nudge). resolveObjection() also falls back to a sibling/related open concern when no exact key matches. This list is injected at runtime, not edited here; tune the wording via the scoring-prompt scaffolding.
0 is the target for ordinary turns. This scorer drives a live satisfaction meter, so routine turns land on 0 — but genuinely good objection-handling moves the needle up, and genuinely bad moves down.

Never penalize ABSENCE. A turn is not at fault merely for lacking a next step, a benefit mention, or an acknowledgement phrase. Judge what the counsellor DID against what a good counsellor would do at THIS moment of THIS phase — not against a checklist.

Phase context matters: Discovery is where the student answers questions; Presentation is a listening phase where short student acks are normal and plain counsellor explaining is fine; Objections is where pushback concentrates and empathy + specifics matter most.
Backchannel words (ok, hmm, haan, ji, theek hai, achha...) are conversational glue. A 1-4 word turn made entirely of these is skipped before the LLM and scores 0.
Edit phaseExpectations to retune what 'good' looks like per phase. Edit severityBands to widen or tighten how far a single turn can move the meter. Keep '0' as the modal band.
counterMoves.reward / counterMoves.penalize are grounded in real converting vs. losing calls — these are the moves worth nudging the score on. Add or remove with care.
